sentences.base_language: Daiki signed up for a cooking class at the culinary school near his apartment, something he had wanted to do for years.
sentences.target_language: 大輝は何年も前からやってみたいと思っていた料理教室に、アパートの近くの料理学校でようやく申し込んだ。
sentences.romanized: Daiki wa nannen mo mae kara yatte mitai to omotte ita ryōri kyōshitsu ni, apāto no chikaku no ryōri gakkō de yōyaku mōshikonda.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜てみたい** = want to try doing: やっ**てみたい** (want to try doing it). **〜てみる** (try doing to see how it goes) + **たい** (want to): 食べ**てみたい** (want to try eating), 行っ**てみたい** (want to try going). The **みる** (look/try) adds a nuance of experimenting or seeing what happens — 'want to give it a go.'
**ようやく** = finally, at long last — used when something is achieved after considerable time or effort: **ようやく**完成した (finally finished). Compare: **やっと** (at last — more emotional relief): **やっと**会えた (finally got to meet you). **ようやく** is slightly more formal and emphasises the length of the wait; **やっと** emphasises the relief of the outcome. **申し込む** (mōshikomu) = to apply for, to sign up for: 授業に**申し込む** (sign up for a class).

sentences.base_language: When he arrived, the instructor — an experienced chef named Michiko — welcomed the group warmly.
sentences.target_language: 大輝が到着すると、ミチコという経験豊かな料理人の講師が、グループ全員を温かく出迎えてくれた。
sentences.romanized: Daiki ga tōchaku suru to, Michiko to iu keiken yutaka na ryōrinin no kōshi ga, gurūpu zen'in wo atatakaku demukaete kureta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜すると** = when ~ happened (and then): 到着する**と** (upon arriving/when he arrived). The **〜と** conditional here expresses a natural or immediate consequence: ドアを開ける**と** 猫がいた (opened the door and there was a cat). It captures the moment of discovery or the immediate result following an action, making it ideal for narrative scenes.
**〜という** = called/named ~: ミチコ**という** 講師 (an instructor named Michiko). The pattern **〜という + noun** is used to define or identify something by its name or label: 「料理教室」**という** 本 (a book called 'Cooking Class'). **出迎える** (demukaeru) = to go out to welcome, to come out to greet — **出** (go out) + **迎える** (welcome/receive): a warmer word than simply **歓迎する** (welcome formally).

sentences.base_language: She announced that they would make a traditional Japanese three-course meal from scratch today.
sentences.target_language: 今日は伝統的な和食のフルコースを一から作ると、ミチコは参加者に伝えた。
sentences.romanized: Kyō wa dentōteki na washoku no furu kōsu wo ichi kara tsukuru to, Michiko wa sankasha ni tsutaeta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜と伝える** = to convey that ~, to tell that ~ (quoted content + と + verb of communication): 作る**と** 伝えた (told them that they would make). The **と** quotation particle works with a plain-form clause to mark reported speech or information: 来る**と** 言った (said he would come), できる**と** 知らせた (informed them it was possible).
**一から** = from scratch, from zero, from the very beginning — **一** (ichi, one/the beginning) + **から** (from): **一から**やり直す (start over from scratch), **一から**学ぶ (learn from the ground up). Compare: **ゼロから** (from zero — same meaning, more modern/casual): **ゼロから**始める (start from zero). **和食** (washoku) = traditional Japanese cuisine — **和** (wa, Japan/Japanese) + **食** (shoku, food/eating): a broad term for the traditional Japanese culinary tradition.

sentences.base_language: Daiki had never cooked anything more complex than instant ramen, so he felt both excited and nervous.
sentences.target_language: インスタントラーメンより複雑なものを料理したことがない大輝は、ワクワクしながらも緊張していた。
sentences.romanized: Insutanto rāmen yori fukuzatsu na mono wo ryōri shita koto ga nai Daiki wa, wakuwaku shinagara mo kinchō shite ita.
literal: インスタントラーメンより
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜たことがない** = have never done ~: 料理した**ことがない** (have never cooked). The pattern **〜たことがある/ない** (have/have never experienced doing) uses the past plain form + **こと** (nominalizer) + **が** + **ある/ない**. This is the standard way to express life experience: 富士山に登った**ことがある** (have climbed Mt. Fuji), 食べた**ことがない** (have never eaten).
**〜ながらも** = even while ~, although ~ at the same time: ワクワクし**ながらも** 緊張していた (was nervous even while feeling excited). **〜ながら** ordinarily expresses simultaneous actions; adding **も** creates a concessive nuance — 'despite one thing, also another.' Compare plain **〜ながら**: 音楽を聴き**ながら** 勉強する (study while listening to music — neutral simultaneous action). **ながらも** suggests a contrast or tension between the two states.

sentences.base_language: First, Michiko demonstrated how to hold a kitchen knife correctly to avoid cutting their fingers.
sentences.target_language: まず、ミチコは指を切らないように包丁の正しい持ち方を実演して見せた。
sentences.romanized: Mazu, Michiko wa yubi wo kiranai yō ni hōchō no tadashii mochi kata wo jitsuen shite miseta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ないように** = so as not to ~, in order to avoid ~: 切ら**ないように** (so as not to cut). **〜ように** expresses purpose or aim; the negative **〜ないように** expresses the aim of preventing something: 遅れ**ないように** 早く出る (leave early so as not to be late), 忘れ**ないように** メモする (make a note so as not to forget).
**〜て見せる** = to do ~ and show (someone), to demonstrate: 実演し**て見せた** (demonstrated and showed them). **〜て見せる** is the pattern for performing an action as a demonstration for others to observe: やっ**て見せる** (show how it's done), 書い**て見せる** (write it out to show you). The **見せる** (show/let see) adds the nuance that the action is performed explicitly for an audience. **持ち方** (mochi kata) = way of holding — **持つ** (hold) + **方** (kata, way/method): a very productive pattern — **食べ方** (way of eating), **使い方** (way of using).

sentences.base_language: She explained that most kitchen accidents happen because people do not pay proper attention.
sentences.target_language: 台所での事故のほとんどは、きちんと注意を払っていないせいで起こると彼女は説明した。
sentences.romanized: Daidokoro de no jiko no hotondo wa, kichinto chūi wo haratte inai sei de okoru to kanojo wa setsumei shita.
literal: 注意を払っていないせいで
sentences.grammarNote:
**注意を払う** = to pay attention, to take care — **注意** (chūi, attention/caution) + **払う** (harau, to pay). A set phrase: **注意を払う** is the standard Japanese equivalent of 'pay attention.' Compare: **気をつける** (ki wo tsukeru, to be careful — more casual/everyday): 足元に**気をつける** (watch your step); **注意する** (chūi suru, to be cautious/to warn). **払う** literally means 'to pay' (money) but combines with **注意** to form this idiom.
**ほとんど** = most, almost all — **ほとんどは** (as for most, the majority): 事故の**ほとんど** (most accidents). **ほとんど** can modify nouns (most) or function adverbially (almost): **ほとんど**終わった (almost finished), **ほとんど**の学生 (most students). Compare: **大半** (taihan, the greater half, majority — slightly more formal): 参加者の**大半** (the majority of participants).

sentences.base_language: Daiki practiced chopping onions and his eyes began to water almost immediately.
sentences.target_language: 大輝が玉ねぎを刻む練習をすると、ほぼすぐに目から涙が出てきた。
sentences.romanized: Daiki ga tamanegi wo kizamu renshū wo suru to, hobo sugu ni me kara namida ga dete kita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜てくる** (directional auxiliary — toward the speaker/present): 涙が出て**きた** (tears came out/started flowing — a change developing and arriving). **〜てくる** marks a change or action that moves toward the speaker or the present moment: 暗くなっ**てきた** (it's been getting darker), 雨が降っ**てきた** (it started raining). Compare **〜ていく** (movement/change away from present): 涙が引いてい**く** (the tears are receding).
**刻む** (kizamu) = to chop finely, to mince, to dice — a culinary verb specific to cutting into small pieces: 玉ねぎを**刻む** (chop onions), にんにくを**刻む** (mince garlic). Compare: **切る** (kiru, to cut — general), **薄切りにする** (usugiri ni suru, to slice thinly), **みじん切りにする** (mijingiri ni suru, to mince finely). **刻む** often implies a rhythmic, repeated chopping motion.

sentences.base_language: The other students laughed kindly, and Michiko said it meant the onions were very fresh.
sentences.target_language: ほかの生徒たちは優しく笑い、ミチコは「玉ねぎがとても新鮮な証拠ですよ」と言った。
sentences.romanized: Hoka no seito-tachi wa yasashiku warai, Michiko wa 「tamanegi ga totemo shinsen na shōko desu yo」 to itta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜な証拠** = proof/sign that ~ (noun + **な** + 証拠): 新鮮**な証拠** (proof of freshness, a sign that it is fresh). **証拠** (shōko) = evidence, proof. The construction **〜ということの証拠** (proof that ~) is often shortened in speech to **〜な証拠** or **〜の証拠**. A natural, everyday expression: 元気**な証拠** (a sign that you're healthy), うまくいっている**証拠** (proof things are going well).
**よ** sentence-final particle for informing: 新鮮な証拠です**よ** (it's a sign they're fresh — I'm telling you this as information). **よ** conveys that the speaker is offering information the listener may not have. In a teaching context like this, **よ** is warm and reassuring rather than blunt: the instructor is gently explaining. Compare **ね** (confirmation-seeking): 新鮮ですよ**ね** (they're fresh, aren't they?).

sentences.base_language: For the main course, they prepared a slow-simmered Japanese stew with seasonal vegetables and tender chicken.
sentences.target_language: メインコースには、旬の野菜と柔らかい鶏肉を使ったじっくり煮込んだ和風シチューを作った。
sentences.romanized: Mein kōsu ni wa, shun no yasai to yawarakai toriniku wo tsukatta jikkuri nikonoda wahū shichū wo tsukutta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**旬** (shun) = the season for something, peak season — a key concept in Japanese food culture: **旬**の魚 (fish in season), **旬**の味 (seasonal flavour). **旬** refers specifically to the time when an ingredient is at its natural best. Compare: **季節の** (kisetsu no, seasonal — general): 季節の野菜 (seasonal vegetables — vaguer, any season). **旬** implies the ingredient is currently at peak ripeness and flavour.
**じっくり** = slowly and carefully, taking one's time — a mimetic adverb: **じっくり**煮込む (simmer long and low), **じっくり**考える (think things over carefully). **じっくり** suggests unhurried, thorough attention: **じっくり**読む (read carefully/slowly). Compare: **ゆっくり** (slowly — general pace), **じっくり** (slowly with patience and thoroughness — implies quality through time). **煮込む** (nikomu) = to simmer, to braise — **煮る** (simmer) + **込む** (into/thoroughly): to cook by simmering into tenderness.

sentences.base_language: Michiko walked around the room, tasting each person's dish and offering quiet suggestions.
sentences.target_language: ミチコは室内を歩き回り、一人ひとりの料理を味見しながら静かにアドバイスを伝えた。
sentences.romanized: Michiko wa shitsunai wo arukimawari, hitori hitori no ryōri wo ajimi shinagara shizuka ni adobaisu wo tsutaeta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ながら** = while doing ~ (simultaneous actions, same subject): 味見し**ながら** アドバイスを伝えた (conveyed advice while tasting). **〜ながら** attaches to the masu-stem and indicates two concurrent actions by the same subject: 音楽を聴き**ながら** 歩く (walk while listening to music). The main action (伝えた) is foregrounded; the **〜ながら** clause is secondary.
**歩き回る** = to walk around, to wander about — **歩く** (walk) + **回る** (go around): moving around a space without a fixed destination. The **〜回る** suffix adds the nuance of circular or repeated movement through a space: 探し**回る** (search all around), 走り**回る** (run around). **一人ひとり** (hitori hitori) = each and every person, one by one — a reduplicated form of **一人** (one person): **一人ひとり**に声をかける (speak to each person individually).

sentences.base_language: Daiki accidentally added too much soy sauce but Michiko told him it was an easy mistake to fix.
sentences.target_language: 大輝は誤って醤油を入れすぎてしまったが、ミチコは「簡単に直せますよ」と教えてくれた。
sentences.romanized: Daiki wa ayamatte shōyu wo iresugite shimatta ga, Michiko wa 「kantan ni naosemasu yo」 to oshiete kureta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜すぎる** = to do too much, to be too ~: 入れ**すぎる** (put in too much). **〜すぎる** attaches to the masu-stem of verbs or the stem of adjectives: 食べ**すぎる** (eat too much), 高**すぎる** (too expensive), 静か**すぎる** (too quiet). It expresses excess beyond an appropriate or comfortable level. Combined with **〜てしまう**: 入れ**すぎてしまった** (ended up putting in too much — accidental excess).
**直せます** = can fix, is fixable — potential form of **直す** (naosu, to fix/correct): **直す** → **直せる** (can fix) → polite **直せます**. The potential form **〜せる/〜できる** expresses ability or possibility: 食べ**られる** (can eat), 書け**る** (can write). **誤って** (ayamatte) = accidentally, by mistake — te-form of **誤る** (ayamaru, to make an error/mistake): a more formal/written word for 'accidentally' than **間違えて** (machigaete).

sentences.base_language: She showed him how to balance the flavor by adding a little mirin and a small amount of sugar.
sentences.target_language: みりんを少しと砂糖をほんの少量加えることで味のバランスを整える方法を教えてくれた。
sentences.romanized: Mirin wo sukoshi to satō wo hon no shōryō kuwaeru koto de aji no baransu wo totonoeru hōhō wo oshiete kureta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ことで** = by doing ~, through the act of ~: 加える**ことで** バランスを整える (balance the flavour by adding). **〜こと** nominalizes the verb; **で** marks the means/method. This pattern is extremely common for explaining how something is achieved: 練習する**ことで** 上手になる (become skilled through practice), 話し合う**ことで** 解決する (resolve by talking it over).
**整える** (totonoeru) = to put in order, to balance, to adjust — a precise and elegant verb: 味を**整える** (adjust/balance the flavour), 体調を**整える** (get one's health in order), 環境を**整える** (set up the environment). Compare: **調整する** (chōsei suru, to adjust — more technical/neutral). **ほんの** = just, merely (a tiny amount) — an adverbial modifier emphasising smallness: **ほんの**少し (just a little), **ほんの**一瞬 (just a moment). **ほんの** strengthens the smallness of what follows.

sentences.base_language: The aroma from the pot filled the kitchen and made everyone's mouth water.
sentences.target_language: 鍋から漂う香りがキッチン中に広がり、みんなの口の中が唾液であふれた。
sentences.romanized: Nabe kara tadayou kaori ga kitchin jū ni hirogari, minna no kuchi no naka ga daeki de afureta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**漂う** (tadayou) = to drift, to waft, to float (of scent, mist, smoke) — an evocative intransitive verb: 香りが**漂う** (the scent drifts/wafts), 霧が**漂う** (the mist drifts). **漂う** is used for things that float or drift gently through the air or water. Compare: **漂う** (drift gently — poetic/elegant), **広がる** (hirogaru, to spread — covers a wider area). Used in sequence here: wafted → spread through the kitchen.
**〜中に広がる** = to spread throughout ~: キッチン**中に**広がる (spread throughout the kitchen). **〜中** (jū/chū) = throughout, all over: 世界**中** (throughout the world), 家**中** (throughout the house), 体**中** (all over the body). The reading **じゅう** (jū) is used when it means 'throughout a space'; **ちゅう** (chū) when meaning 'during a time': 一日**中** (all day long). **あふれる** (afureru) = to overflow, to be brimming with: 涙が**あふれる** (tears overflow), 喜びで**あふれる** (overflow with joy).

sentences.base_language: By the time the food was ready, Daiki's arms were tired from all the stirring.
sentences.target_language: 料理ができあがるころには、ずっとかき混ぜ続けていたせいで大輝の腕はくたくたになっていた。
sentences.romanized: Ryōri ga dekiagaru koro ni wa, zutto kakimazete tsuzukete ita sei de Daiki no ude wa kutakuta ni natte ita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜ころには** = by the time ~: できあがる**ころには** (by the time the food was ready). **ころ** (approximate time) + **に** + **は** (topic/contrast): marks a point in time by which something had already occurred. Compare: **〜までには** (by the time of — deadline): 六時**までには** 帰る (be home by six). **ころ** is more approximate and narrative; **まで** is a harder deadline.
**かき混ぜ続ける** = to keep stirring continuously: **かき混ぜる** (kakimazeru, to stir/mix) + **続ける** (tsuzukeru, to continue). The **〜続ける** construction (keep doing ~) attaches to the masu-stem: 食べ**続ける** (keep eating), 歩き**続ける** (keep walking). **くたくた** is a mimetic word expressing total exhaustion: 体が**くたくた**だ (the body is utterly worn out). Often used for physical tiredness after sustained effort.

sentences.base_language: For dessert, they made a simple matcha pudding that required careful attention to temperature.
sentences.target_language: デザートには、温度に細かく気を配る必要があるシンプルな抹茶プリンを作った。
sentences.romanized: Dezāto ni wa, ondo ni komakaku ki wo kubaru hitsuyō ga aru shinpuru na maccha purin wo tsukutta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**気を配る** = to pay careful attention to, to be attentive to — **気** (ki, mind/spirit) + **を配る** (wo kubaru, distribute/attend): to direct one's attention carefully across something. Compare: **気をつける** (ki wo tsukeru, to be careful — more about avoiding danger), **気を配る** (ki wo kubaru, to attend thoughtfully to details/people). **細かく気を配る** (attend to fine details) is a natural, elegant combination.
**〜必要がある** = there is a need to ~, it is necessary to ~: 気を配る**必要がある** (it is necessary to pay attention). The pattern **〜する必要がある** expresses objective necessity: 申し込む**必要がある** (it is necessary to apply), 確認する**必要がある** (it is necessary to confirm). Compare: **〜なければならない** (must do — stronger obligation/duty): 行か**なければならない** (must go). **必要がある** is softer and more factual.

sentences.base_language: Daiki felt a surge of pride when his pudding set perfectly and looked just like the instructor's.
sentences.target_language: プリンが完璧に固まり、講師のものとそっくりになったとき、大輝は胸が誇らしさでいっぱいになった。
sentences.romanized: Purin ga kanpeki ni katamari, kōshi no mono to sokkuri ni natta toki, Daiki wa mune ga hokorashisa de ippai ni natta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**そっくり** = exactly alike, identical in appearance — a mimetic な-adjective: 親に**そっくり** (the spitting image of one's parent), 本物と**そっくり** (exactly like the real thing). **〜とそっくり** = looks just like ~: 先生のもの**とそっくり** (looks just like the teacher's). Compare: **〜と同じ** (the same as — factual/neutral), **そっくり** (identical in appearance — more vivid and expressive).
**胸がいっぱいになる** = to be overcome with emotion, heart swelling: **胸** (mune, chest/heart) + **が** + **いっぱい** (full) + **になる** (become). This is a powerful set phrase for an emotion that fills the chest — pride, gratitude, or joy: 感謝で**胸がいっぱい**になった (was overwhelmed with gratitude). **誇らしさ** (hokorashisa) = pride, a sense of pride — nominalized from **誇らしい** (hokorashii, proud/glowing with pride): **誇らしさ**を感じる (feel a sense of pride).

sentences.base_language: The class sat down together to eat the meal they had prepared, feeling a warm sense of accomplishment.
sentences.target_language: クラス全員が一緒に座り、自分たちで作った料理を温かな達成感とともに味わった。
sentences.romanized: Kurasu zen'in ga issho ni suwari, jibun-tachi de tsukutta ryōri wo atatakana tasseikan to tomo ni ajiwatta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜とともに** = together with ~, along with ~ — a formal/literary conjunction expressing accompaniment: 達成感**とともに** 食べる (eat with a sense of accomplishment). **〜とともに** can also express simultaneous change: 時代**とともに** 変わる (change with the times). Compare: **〜と一緒に** (issho ni, together with — casual/concrete), **〜とともに** (tomo ni — more formal, often used with abstract concepts).
**味わう** (ajiwau) = to savour, to taste with appreciation, to experience deeply — more than just eating: **味わう** implies taking time to appreciate flavour and experience. Compare: **食べる** (taberu, eat — neutral physical action), **味わう** (ajiwau, savour — attentive, appreciative eating or experiencing). **自分たちで** = by themselves, on their own: **自分** (self) + **たち** (plural) + **で** (by/through — indicating agency): by their own efforts, with their own hands. **達成感** (tasseikan) = sense of accomplishment — **達成** (achievement) + **感** (feeling/sense).

sentences.base_language: Daiki discovered that cooking was more satisfying than he had expected.
sentences.target_language: 大輝は、料理が思っていたよりもずっと充実感のあるものだと気がついた。
sentences.romanized: Daiki wa, ryōri ga omotte ita yori mo zutto jūjitsukan no aru mono da to ki ga tsuita.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜よりも** = more than ~, even more than ~: 思っていた**よりも** ずっと (far more than he had thought). **より** is the comparison particle ('than'); **も** adds emphasis — 'even more than.' Compare: **〜より** (than — neutral), **〜よりも** (than — more emphatic). **ずっと** here means 'by far, much' rather than 'always': **ずっと** 大きい (much bigger), **ずっと** おいしい (far more delicious).
**気がつく** = to realize, to notice, to become aware: 料理が好きだと**気がついた** (realized that he liked cooking). Compare: **気づく** (kizuku — same meaning, more modern/casual): 間違いに**気づく** (notice a mistake). **気がつく** tends to be slightly more formal/literary. The difference is subtle; both are widely used. **充実感** (jūjitsukan) = a sense of fulfilment, satisfaction with how one has spent one's time or effort — **充実** (full/enriched) + **感** (feeling): 仕事に**充実感**を感じる (feel a sense of fulfilment in one's work).

sentences.base_language: Michiko invited them all to come back for the next class, which would focus on regional Japanese cuisine.
sentences.target_language: ミチコは全員に、次回は日本の郷土料理に焦点を当てた授業に戻ってくるよう誘ってくれた。
sentences.romanized: Michiko wa zen'in ni, jikai wa Nihon no kyōdo ryōri ni shōten wo ateta jugyō ni modotte kuru yō sasoute kureta.
sentences.grammarNote:
**〜よう(に)** (invitation/instruction nuance): 戻ってくる**よう** 誘う (invite to come back). When **〜よう(に)** follows a verb and precedes a verb of communication (言う、頼む、誘う), it creates an indirect request or instruction: 早く来る**よう**に 頼んだ (asked to come quickly), 休む**よう**に 言った (told to rest). This is the standard pattern for reported requests in Japanese.
**郷土料理** (kyōdo ryōri) = regional cuisine, local speciality dishes — **郷土** (kyōdo, hometown/local area) + **料理** (ryōri, cuisine). Each Japanese region has its own **郷土料理**: Kyushu's tonkotsu ramen, Osaka's okonomiyaki, Hokkaido's soup curry. Compare: **地方料理** (chihō ryōri, regional food — more neutral/generic). **郷土** carries a nostalgic, rooted feeling of connection to one's home region. **焦点を当てる** (shōten wo ateru) = to focus on — **焦点** (focus/focal point) + **を当てる** (to direct/aim at): a formal expression for focusing on a topic.

sentences.base_language: Daiki went home with a full stomach and a new passion, already planning what he would cook the following weekend.
sentences.target_language: 大輝はお腹をいっぱいにして、新たな情熱を胸に家路についた。来週末に何を料理するかはもう考えていた。
sentences.romanized: Daiki wa onaka wo ippai ni shite, arata na jōnetsu wo mune ni kairoji ni tsuita. Raishūmatsu ni nani wo ryōri suru ka wa mō kangaete ita.
literal: お腹をいっぱいにして、
sentences.grammarNote:
**家路につく** = to set off for home, to make one's way home — **家路** (kairoji, the road home) + **につく** (set out on): a literary compound for the journey homeward. **〜路につく** is a set expression: **家路**につく (head for home), **旅路**につく (set off on a journey). Compare: **家に帰る** (ie ni kaeru, go home — plain/everyday). **家路につく** has a warmer, more evocative tone, suggesting contentment at the end of an outing.
**〜かは** = as for what/whether ~ (embedded question as topic): 何を料理する**かは** もう考えていた (as for what to cook, he was already thinking about it). The pattern **〜か + は/が/を** embeds a question clause as a grammatical noun phrase: 誰が来る**かは** わからない (I don't know who will come), どこへ行く**かを** 決めた (decided where to go). This is the standard way to nominalize a question in Japanese.
